nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving streams and tributaries with sandy substrate and leaf litter

diet.notes: The Pygmy Cory's mouth is very small, so standard fish food is often too large. Crushed flakes, micropellets, sinking micro wafers, and frozen baby brine shrimp, micro worms, and Artemia nauplii (newly hatched brine shrimp) all work well. Unlike most cories, this species tends not to forage effectively at the bottom and generally does better with targeted mid-water feeding.

avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Birger A; 978x601. Tack-sharp mid-water swimming shot showing iridescent silvery body and bold black snout-to-tail lateral stripe against a dark natural background; clean, no watermark. Mid-water pose matches this species' unusual schooling habit.

Photographer Joel Carnat; 1024x590. Two individuals together over leaf litter and sand, illustrating the social/shoaling behaviour; both show the diagnostic lateral stripe. Minor caveat: thin title banner and faint edge watermark.
Photographer Anastasiya Lvova; 1881x1081. Clean single specimen over sand, whole fish in frame with clear lateral stripe and barbels; no watermark. Slight softness on the tail.

summary: The Pygmy Cory is one of the smallest members of the corydoras family, reaching just 2–3 cm. Unlike most cories, it tends to school actively in mid-water rather than the bottom. Groups of 10 or more are generally advised for confident, natural behavior.
careNotes: Unlike most bottom-dwelling Corydoras, the Pygmy Cory tends to school at mid-water level, so open swimming space above the substrate is important. Groups of 10 or more are generally advised — smaller groups have been known to become shy and retreat to cover. Their mouths are very small, and standard sinking wafers are often too large; crushed or micro-sized foods are needed. The species is sensitive to copper-based medications, so any fish treatment containing copper should be avoided in tanks where they are kept. A fine sand substrate is preferred, though the Pygmy Cory spends less time foraging the bottom than larger cory species. Like other members of the family, the Pygmy Cory is a facultative air breather (meaning it can supplement gill breathing by gulping atmospheric air) and has been documented periodically surfacing to do so. Sharp pectoral spines can catch in nets; a container is recommended for catching them. Two closely related species — the Hasta Cory (Gastrodermus hastatus) and the Salt-and-Pepper Cory (Gastrodermus habrosus) — share the small body and mid-water schooling habit; the Hasta Cory is distinguished by a spot on the caudal peduncle (the narrow base of the tail) rather than a lateral stripe. The species was formerly known as Corydoras pygmaeus; the older name is still common in the aquarium trade.
breedingNotes: The Pygmy Cory has been bred in captivity with moderate success. Conditioning the fish with live and frozen foods tends to encourage spawning, and a deliberate temperature drop — mimicking the cool-season rainfall that triggers breeding in the wild — has been reported to initiate spawning events. The female holds 2–4 eggs between her pelvic fins while they are fertilized, then attaches them individually to plant leaves, the aquarium glass, or other surfaces. Clutches typically run around 50–100 eggs, which generally hatch in approximately 3–5 days. Fry are very small at first and do best on infusoria or commercial fry foods, graduating to micro worms and Artemia nauplii (newly hatched brine shrimp) as they grow.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range, taxonomy, temperature, and pH. FishBase lists max SL as 2.1 cm (standard length); SeriouslyFish gives 3 cm total length — both consistent with the 2–3 cm total-length range used here. FishBase pH range of 6.0–8.0 is wider than SeriouslyFish's 6.4–7.4; used 6.0–7.5 per cross-source average and user brief.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Confirmed mid-water swim behavior, 10-gallon (38L) minimum, school of 6+ as baseline; 60L used here to support a school of 10+ as recommended by specialist sources.
